intent: Four denials across exotic object classes (key, fd, dbus, kernel_service), each with class-specific perm vocabulary the parser must pass through verbatim. Four separate triples -> four separate allow lines, no cross-class union.
expected_narrow_allow: allow crond_t self:key { read search write };
_kinds_note: authoritative_category in {TeAllowable, Constraint, Bounds} (libsepol/audit2why replay); floor_label in {none, Permissive, MlsSuspected, RoleSuspected} (record-only heuristic). For decline scenarios these MAY disagree on purpose.

oracle.audit2allow_N: allow crond_t self:key { read search write }; allow httpd_t init_t:fd use; allow NetworkManager_t system_dbusd_t:dbus send_msg; allow init_t kernel_t:kernel_service use_as_override;
oracle.audit2allow_R: dbus_send_system_bus(NetworkManager_t); init_use_fds(httpd_t); allow crond_t self:key { read search write }; allow init_t kernel_t:kernel_service use_as_override;
oracle.narrower_than_R_by: audit2allow -R over-broadens the dbus and fd denials to interface macros (dbus_send_system_bus(), init_use_fds()) but leaves the key self-grant and the kernel_service grant as raw allows (no covering macro exists for those exotic classes). Our expected.te emits all four as raw narrow allows - matching -N exactly and narrower than -R on the two macro-substituted triples.
oracle.audit2why_note: key/fd/dbus replay as 'would be allowed by active policy' (already-granted TE shape); kernel_service replays as a clean 'Missing TE allow'. All four are TeAllowable. Each exotic class uses its own perm vocabulary: key {read write search}, fd {use}, dbus {send_msg}, kernel_service {use_as_override} - all verified present in the policy class definitions.

triage_expectation.suspected_trap: A parser that only understands file/dir/process classes will fail to pass through exotic perms (key:search, fd:use, dbus:send_msg, kernel_service:use_as_override) verbatim, or may union perms across the four DIFFERENT classes into one rule. Correct output is four separate triple-scoped allows, each with its class-specific perm set passed through unchanged. The key denial is a self-check (scontext==tcontext -> 'self').
